I had a chance to catch up on your chat with Andy a bit.
Quick on me, I've been in growth.
I've in growth.
been I've
I've in digital a very long time now.
It's honestly starting to make me feel old as I have to think back and tell the story.
It's now been, I think this has been my 21st year.
I started in agency side, spent 10 years there, very small agency in Rhode Island at the time, and then moved out to California state agency side for a bit, went in-house for eight or nine years.
And a lot of that was leading growth at a variety of different stages and stops.

And while my agency experience first started in like paid and analytics and testing, really started to, I'd say like the theme of my in-house career was companies that need to diversify their channels.
They're super stuck on just like paid search and they don't know how to like stand up other repeatable paid channels.
They need to grow an organic engine or at least scale it.
And so that is kind of was the theme of my career that brought me to leading growth at a company called Homebase, which actually happened.

I to be the first growthx customer, and then I joined growthx a little over a year ago, and had been helping lead on the delivery side for a while, spent a shortstop in sales, helping us get Marcel out of the weeds there.
Then we had a director who left, so I helped kind of take on his pod for a bit or a few pods.

And so, anyway, so, yeah, it seems like you have a great background.
Objective of today is really just to understand that at a deeper level and how you think, and, of course, we'll give you time for questions at the end.
Sure.
Cool.
Well, kicking it off, what would be helpful for me is just a, could be, you know.

Either the most fun, impactful, however you want to define it, but just something that really stands out as a project, or maybe not even an individual project, but an impact that you were able to drive for one of your clients that really, really stands out.
And we'd love to just kind of like dig into it and understand kind of from beginning to point of impact what that evolution looked like.
Yeah, for that, I'll focus on like a project.
It's actually an ongoing effort right now, too.
But, and I hope you don't mind.
Do you mind if I use client names?
know like it's a lot.
I mean, up to you, man.
It is not a requirement.
Everyone handles that in different ways.
I won't share beyond this being recorded.

I'll just, I'll let it release.

So the client's Chanel.
So working in like the luxury space, just based on my background, like I've done, you know, kind of like every different.
Like
Industry, think.
then luxury was my first kind of, or my first experience with them was working here at Assembly.
And luxury is a different beast, right?
So luxury is, they're very, I don't know, they're not super receptive to a lot of like different challenging ideas outside of like, just focusing on what the core brand emission is.
They don't like to take a lot of risks.
Like they like to, their goals are always like, hey, let's lock in the highest percentage of like branded SOB we can, increasing branded click-through rates, things like that, right?
So from an SEO strategy standpoint, it's not always like the most appealing and it can kind of feel like, you know, you're pulling people by their teeth, right?

But something we've been pushing for years, you know, for several years is that during reporting, you know, we'll constantly report on, like, I think it's like 99, 98% of their clicks are from like branded search, right?
So a lot of times just interest is what shifts their performance, right?
As long as there's not like a major SERP change, right?
So what I've been drilling to them for ages now is, hey, we need to really increase

It's like what we're driving from a non-brand perspective because if fluctuation occurs in your search interest, if behavioral changes due to economic factors change, whatever it might be, we're just heavily relying on that.

So the project itself was to, at scale, let's create a ton of different PLPs to target a lot of these non-branded variation terms that they hadn't been historically, as well as improving some longer-tail branded terms.
So what we did for them, you know, eventually we got buy-in.
We kind of had to do some pilot tests a little bit here and there to eventually get buy-in.
But effectively, we got buy-in from them to work with an outside vendor.

We handled the research on our end in terms of mapping out the different product categories they have, the different inventory levels they have, understanding their client base and kind of how they're searching and what attributes they search for in relation to their products.

And then coming up with that comprehensive plan to launch, develop these plans, optimize those pages, launch those pages, optimize those pages, and let them continue to grow to a point then where we repeat that across divisions across their website.
So those launched and just one, one, getting the buy-in was a win because of what I previously told you about them never wanting to do anything.
But then two, the success that we've seen from there has been awesome, right?
Like, so one of the goals that really, you know, them and then also, you know, they were super favorable was, again, that increase in branded click-through rate.

So, like, I know our goal was still the non-branded approach, but it still, like, made them excited that, like, we were still achieving their previous goal, but they didn't even know, like, that was an obtainable or, like, how to get there necessarily without the content we had recommended.
And then the second piece was that non-branded growth of, like, you know, again, I'll just use a simplistic keyword that we may be targeting.
It would be a little bit deeper than this, but let's say, like, red lipstick, right?
Let's say they had a lipstick page, but they didn't have color variations.
They didn't have material.
They didn't have site, whatever it might be.
So creating all of those, seeing that visibility and growth come from there, seeing revenue come from there, that's been an ongoing project, which we've now, because of having that time frame.
And reporting on the success we've seen, there are other divisions of their site.
And just to give you a breakdown, they have like, they break their website down by like product categories in a sense of like, that's who owns and operates a section of their site.
So they all kind of, they work in like the same branded guidelines, but they all have different kind of strategy approaches and like different appetites for certain things.
So in our like core business, like quarterly business reviews, like we'll talk about the success of this project and then other of these divisions would have been interested.
So much so that we've now expanded those conversations with the vendor that now we're getting more buy-in and now the whole research phase is starting over again.

So I know that was a long kind of winded way to get around that, but I kind of wanted to give you some of the background information as to why it kept me excited and why I was so pumped on the success.
Very cool.
So walk me through a little bit of like that, that period of getting their buy-in, like how did you tell that story?
You know, it sounded like that it took a little bit of iteration.
So how did you think about that?
Yeah, so honestly, it was a lot of, you know, we'll do like monthly reporting, then we'll have like the quarterly reviews, right?

So a lot of the times when things are good, right, a lot of people maybe don't care as much necessarily about some of the recommendations we're making or not enough questions are being asked, I think.
So previously, you know, they had a lot of branded growth interest just because of how people were searching, right, or like new product releases, things of that nature.
But we had always echoed in the background, like, hey, this isn't necessarily sustainable, or there's other areas in which, you know, you're not seeing growth.
Let's use the lipsticks, for example, maybe Chanel lipstick wasn't seeing as much interest, but Chanel handbags was so like that interest was outweighing the decreases there.
So it didn't become as much of an important aspect to focus on.

But then what happened was, is, and normally, this is kind of like the biggest issue or pain point from us, but I actually thought it was a great thing that happened was the amount of changes we were seeing in the SERPs, especially on Google, and like how much of the diminishing impact that was having on organic.
It was like really cutting into the performances we were seeing.
So then, you know, things started to get a little negative and then people were like, hey, what's going on?

And so it was more so reiterating the story we were telling, showing that impact, showing like clear visual representations of what that looks like and saying, you know, throwing that out to say like, hey, like, again, this isn't sustainable.
And now we have less of a piece of the pie to really grab from.
So we need to put in an action plan here.
So that got the interest.

And then honestly, the evolution of AI and the adaptation of AI honestly accelerated that even further to where more people were like, you know, like even outside of that project for creating those different landing pages to target different attributes, Chanel had historically never wanted to have a content plan.
Right.

So like we've been we've been preaching these things and it just kind of took like, again, reiterating the numbers, telling that story and then like really pinpointing it and showing like what it could be.
And so with, you know, the evolution of AI, now we actually have these standups recently going on and now we're starting to map out like a content plan, a content hub and things like that.

So, yeah, it just goes down to, again, the storytelling and then, like, building that relationship and trust with the client so that they, like, one, they know you're an expert, but two, like, they know you're steering them in the right direction.
And so that storyline during the approach and then the storyline throughout, like, you the constant check-ins of saying, this is what we're seeing.
This is the progress we've seen.
This is what we think based upon what we saw in this division and what we can do for this division.
And, again, just kind of reiterating those expectations.
How, what was the scope of the engagement before that?
Like, was it, you know, just helping ensure, like, technical SEO on-page things?
Like, what did it look pre that work?
Pretty limited.
So, the other thing that gets, it's one of our largest accounts, which sounds silly that it would be limited in what we're doing.
But that goes back to what I was saying around how Luxury is so adverse to doing a lot of things, right?
So, a lot of what we would do previously was, like, they have, yeah, and, yeah.
I, yeah, it's
Maybe I'm getting a little too far into how their business is structured, but I think it applies, right?
So they have each marketing team on a regional level, but then they have their own global department in France because of where their headquarters at.
So basically, you have to get buy-in from the U.S.
team and then buy-in from the global team.
The global team is pretty hesitant to do a lot of things, I think, just because they have their own separate roadmaps.
They've got all these different regions going on, and if they do something for one region, they probably have to apply it to others and blah, blah, blah.
But anyway, prior to that, we were doing a lot of, you know, we could do some technical audits, a lot of technical reviews, a lot of reviews of, like, releases that were happening.
They do have, like, ongoing content additions that happen to the site with, like, new product releases or just new campaigns they've launched that will, like, help to optimize.
There'll be reviews of, like, some off-site things around, like, their local presence.
And then, you know, maybe we, throughout just, like, reporting, identifying, like, the top opportunities to improve optimizations and, like, thinking about, like, ways to scale that through,

I don't know, metadata rules that they could apply to each product offering or like certain attributes that need to be definitely promoted on products or like the structured data they're implementing.
So it was definitely a frustrating time because it's like we would do a lot, but like it didn't feel like a lot was getting done.
But like with this, it was like, you know, actually creating net new copy, creating net new pages and like seeing the impact driven from this.
So sounds like you were able to help solidify the relationship really a lot, making sure you're providing real value.
Which, you know, makes a lot of sense.

It's always good to, you know, at the, excuse me, at times we certainly have, you know, different versions of the same thing where, you know, if you're just going off the vibes or whatever, and, you know, the signals you can see in and around a meeting, you know, it can, it can appear healthier.
But yeah, finding those like, well, are we actually providing that impact and how is this going to be felt later on and thinking down the road?
I think it's really smart.
Yeah, and I think you definitely want to get ahead of that before people start asking.
What value are you bringing, know, so to your point.
Okay.
Yeah.
I certainly was able to, I think, infer this based off what you shared, but can you help me understand, like, the metric you were trying to move through that effort and what the impact of that was?
And I have a couple other questions related to that.
Sure.
So the, I mean, they're always, all of our clients, well, most of our clients are e-commerce, so their main metric is always, like, revenue, right?

So obviously we'll focus on, like, those, I don't know what they're, like, standard, but, like, those foundational elements of, like, how many sessions are you going, how many revenue, or how much revenue are you driving?

And then we would also, you know, again, I would break down, I would break down reporting on, like, the branded and non-branded side from both, like, a click performance and then as well as rankings to show, like, visibility and traffic from them.
And then also creating, like, blended reports of those same metrics.
So, like, that really helps from the branded side of things because a lot of times what their, what their global team or some of the team.
members would say, right, is that, again, I'll go back to that lipstick example I gave earlier, was that they'd be like, hey, we have a page for Chanel lipstick.
Like, I don't necessarily think we need to create content for the different color variations.
Like, we're still going to rank well for our own branded terms.
It's probably not going to lead to much incremental growth.
But what we were able to do is actually create these blended reports in which we were saying, all right, hey, this is the keyword, and this is your performance during the time frame before we did it.
This is the uptick we've actually seen since then, whether it's, you click-through rate, improved ranking, improved share of voice, whatever that might be.
So we've, like, sprinkled in those numbers throughout to really help tell that story a little bit better.
Because, again, that's kind of, like, the difference between saying, like, this is the movement we saw versus, like, this is the value we created.
Yeah, makes sense.
Answered some of my other questions.
That's perfect.
Love the efficiency.
Looking back on that work, what is something that stands out if you were to do it over again that you may have handled differently, a failure?
you will, into getting them to that point of diversifying their brand on brand.

So one issue that we found is that the way that the vendor would work was that, you know, we would effectively, they wanted us to handle the research just because we'd worked with previous vendors across their account and then other accounts.
And like some of these, and I don't know how familiar you are working with like some of these like large scale PLP creation types of companies.
But a lot of times it almost feels like they just do like an export from SEMrush and then they go like, you don't rank for a random color.
You know, there was one instance in which they had, you know, we were trying to improve like perfume performance, right?
And like rose is like a scent of the perfume.
And one of the companies suggested we try to improve our ranking visibility for the term rose, which I was like, that doesn't make any.
So regardless, we took on, we took on like the, the keyword research.
But the issue we eventually ran into is the way the vendor works, right?
Is that, you know, we create all of these different keywords and targeting and kind of map out the attributes.
And then they take the product data feed and then effectively align those together to try to do it at a more scaling and efficient rate.
So if we say, you know, we're creating a red lipstick page, they can easily identify these are all lipsticks.
These are all ones that are marked as red.
What we found out was that Chanel's product data feed was super limited and not to any follow-up of our own.
It's not like we're the ones handling their product data feed.

That's more of an internal thing they've realized is that the more comprehensive and the more they can fulfill this data feed, the better it is for long-term for this type of work when we start to map out all these different variations of keywords.
That way we don't, because what ended up happening is that in instances like that, we had to potentially go in manually and kind of check for things.

And we found some workarounds, right, for like crawling the site, doing custom extractions for like product details and mapping one-to-one, but could have been done a lot more efficiently had the data feed just been properly filled out.
Makes sense.
Cool.

B2B SaaS and my previous agency, we had a couple of those clients, more so like it was B2B on the, I believe it was like B2B insurance related.
I forget exactly what their niche was, but yeah, we had a couple of clients on that side.

And a lot of that agreement was around, you know, creating the types of content or creating content roadmaps for them and creating content on their end, helping to, you know, optimize that content as well as like keeping up with their like current content or their overall content library and just continuing to like optimize and further optimize that.
So I'd say it's relatively fresh and continues to do well.
Got it.
Got it.

Outside of like, you know, PLP optimization, you know, product description type things, maybe even within e-commerce, what is your typical mix of like how you're, you know, what type of content you're optimizing and what you're trying to influence for your clients?
Sure.
So for going back to Chanel, they're, they're definitely primarily just like PLP, PDP.
Sometimes they release things, are called like mood boards, which are like long form content, like overview promotional content around their products.
One content or client I was going to talk about, but I didn't want to talk about it yet because it's not launched yet.
So I didn't feel like it was a successful product, but it's like, it's replacing that Chanel project in the back of my mind.
It's like, what I'm most excited about is our client ACE hardware.
And so.
So Ace Hardware has historically always just been PDP, PLP mix, mainly on the PLP side of terms of what we're optimizing.
But what's exciting is that, you know, I think we've had them as a client for 10 years.
I've been on the account for two to three, and we officially just got buy-in for them to launch their content hub.

So right now what we're producing in the background, it's not live yet, but now we're investing a lot more into the different research-oriented content to really go after different users at different stages of their journey, right?
So, you know, them deciding on what type of grill they need, doing the comparisons between grill types, maintenance of the grill, whatever it might be along that product stage.

Because, you know, what we found out is that they do exceptionally well on these different product-slash-purchase-intent types of queries and keywords just because of having that thorough and optimized content on the site.
But where they're really losing ground is those larger competitive stores, whether it's Home Depot,
Things like that.
They have full-blown content production.
They are optimizing their how to use X products.
You're painting your bedroom.
These are the products you'll need.
This is how to do it X, Y, and Z.
So right now we're starting to invest a lot more time into that.
Again, it hasn't launched yet, so I can't necessarily speak to the improvement we're seeing.

But the thought there is not only an organic search, you know, appearing to the different stages, also thinking about AI search and, you know, kind of just how people search within that realm and appearing there.
And then also maybe like the logic behind like, you know, rising ties, lift all ships, right?
So if we have that type of informational research content, theoretically, that should boost a lot of their product copy as well.
Yeah, it makes a lot of sense.
I mean, you clearly know your stuff.
I'd say my assumption, whether right or wrong, there probably could be like some sort of like learning curve that, you know, that is not necessarily like it.

A concern at this point, but like because we skew very heavy B2B SaaS, I think probably the type of strategies that we do is probably a little bit different, but I have a hunch there's a decent lot that's transferable in that.
And, you know, probably where it looks a little bit different is like, obviously, we're not going to be doing a lot of product-based things, although, yeah, you know, that can certainly change.
And I think in common, there's elements of that, and as we think about PSEO type things, that is probably highly transferable, right?
Like some of the work we're doing with Lovable, which may not be, you know, conventionally e-commerce, but I would imagine some of the elements can be thought of in a similar fashion.
But a lot of our content does tend to ski more like towards blogs and other forms of content and like thinking through that kind of strategies, just for context of how I think about it.
But I do think at the same time, though, like we have to be careful because like it's very easy to fall in the trap of like, hey, you've identified these opportunities.
How do you go after them?
create these blogs.
And it's not always the answer.
And so anyways, just sharing a bit of like a little bit what's on my mind related to that.
So yeah, as we go through it, just for context, if there's anything in addition, you know, based off your experience, that's good to like add in the mix to give me that context.
That'd be helpful.
Sure, of course.
Transition a little bit.
So as you're thinking about building a strategy for an account, how do you think about blending in the, you know, let's say we have an account that's coming to you that needs some work, right?
It's maybe, you know, we're just like performance is just kind of meh.
It's, you know, we've been working with them long enough where we should have driven a lot more impact than we have.
And we're brought into the mix to say, hey, like, help us understand what's happening and like how we get some momentum.
And so as you think about that, like, how would you?
Think about the short and the long-term as you're trying to address what we're trying to build a strategy that's going to address those issues that are bubbling up.
Sure.
I was smiling during it because literally at my agency now, I don't know.
It's obviously a common one.
I think we've all probably heard of that.
Yeah.
But my manager was referring to me the other day as the client guy because basically for Chanel and Ace, that's what happened, right?
They were long-tenured relationships in which they weren't going well, they were stagnant, and then I was effectively brought on to kind of help in that area.
So I was just smiling because that type of stuff excites me, but then number two, I think I excel on that.
So from the first standpoint is what I really like to do is kind of dive into what the story that's being told has been historically, right?

A lot of times I feel like we're not – and this might be oversimplifying a lot of times, but we might think that a relationship's not going well just because we're not telling the story that they want to hear.
story here.
So connecting with the client, saying, what are the pain points?
What are your frustrations?
Where do you really want to see?
What are the questions that people above you are asking you that is kind of making this relationship feel a little stale or that we're not actually adding value?
So once I feel like we've addressed that, then I think we'll build out and refine that report, that storytelling, whatever needs to occur to at least have that baseline be established and improved.
So I feel like that helps immensely.
And then from, you know, if things are stagnant, let's say all things KPIs are good, story is good, we've already locked that in.
Then it's about, you know, let's focus on what's not working.
And let's see, you know, is it just because the tactics not as impactful as we thought it might be for external factors?
Is it not, are we not doing it at scale enough to actually have an impact on the top line level?
And if that's the case, you know, how do we scale that further?
Or, you know, where can we shift to actually?
We start to see improvements.

Like, for example, for Ace, what we recently saw was, you know, historically, what we've been doing is that they've been doing, I don't know, 40 to 50 PLPs a month as, like, ongoing content optimization, right?
And that's great in, like, isolated areas, but we don't see that necessarily ladder up to, like, the top line metrics, right?
Because it's having such a small impact.
have hundreds of thousands of different SKUs.
So it's, like, how do we take things that we know are foundationally good and then ladder them up to make actual value and make change, right?
So, you know, leveraging things like AI to increase our output by, right now, I think we're at, like, 20 to 30% increases.

And then also, like, leveraging that philosophy and, like, we're going to optimize all these PLPs, but we're also going to use this repeatable approach to optimize PDPs that fall under those PLPs, right?
Again, kind of, like, ladder up combined effect of, like, really optimizing more content that's related to each other.
And then when we start to think about that content.
We're mapping out in the background, like tying back the key product categories to that content hub and that content strategy and kind of mixing those all together, right?
So launching those, you know, at great seasonal times and then having those optimized at the same time.
So hopefully we have that like larger impact of event or impactful kind of strategy.
And then, again, just kind of repeating some of those scalable opportunities that we can do across other tactics, like whether it's image all tag optimization at scale or whatever it might be.
And really just dumping that into like that area to try to help improve things.
Cool.
Cool.
Certainly what adds complexity as you think about that nowadays is what's happening with the AEO, GEO, whatever you want to call it.
But how do you think about that now as you're doing your typical, you know, SEO planning?
Where does AEO fit in the mix?
How do you work it into the mix to ensure you're, I'm sure your clients are asking about it.
A lot of our clients are asking.
So yeah, no, it's, it's top of every conversation, right?
It's the shiniest new toy.
We have, so first, to start from the foundational approach, right?
When we get to like the storytelling and research, you know, we've, we've brought on prompt tracking and things like that.
We've built out Google Analytics reports that have referring traffic from those AI platforms and kind of what the performance looks like from those.
So we have that.
But then there's also the point of like getting clients up to speed and kind of learning and resetting expectations of what this looks like, right?
It took so long to, I feel like, get them up to standard, at least get them to an understanding of like traditional SEO metrics and kind of how everything effectively functions.
And, you know, there's a lot of carryover with that, but also a lot of things need to get thrown out the door.
At least again, expectations need to get reset in terms of like, all because we populate for a prompt doesn't necessarily mean we're going to see traffic and revenue from said visibility, right?
So there's that whole performance monitoring and then expectation leveling.
And then in terms of the, you know, how we're prioritizing.
